title: Entropy bounds for reduced density matrices of fermionic states
speaker: Elliott Lieb

proof_stages.label: Fermionic minimum-entanglement problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of quantifying unavoidable entanglement in antisymmetric n-fermion states, proposes Slater determinants as the expected minimizers, and explains that finite n is fixed by the physically relevant algebra of observables.

proof_stages.label: Bipartite entropy framework and mutual-information bound
proof_stages.summary: After recalling separability, e-bits, and entropy-based entanglement measures, the first theorem gives a fermionic lower bound on the mutual information of a two-particle reduction, with equality exactly for pure n-particle Slater determinants.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative subadditivity proof with antisymmetric projection
proof_stages.summary: A general quantitative subadditivity estimate is proved using Peierls-type and Golden-Thompson inequalities, and then antisymmetry is inserted via the two-particle antisymmetric projector to obtain the Slater equality case.

proof_stages.label: Entanglement of formation lower bound
proof_stages.summary: The convex-roof definition of entanglement of formation is applied to fermionic two-body states, proving E_F >= log 2 with equality precisely for convex combinations of two-particle Slater determinants, hence giving a faithful fermionic excess-entanglement measure.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic entropy of two-particle reductions
proof_stages.summary: For the entropy S(rho_12) of the two-particle reduced density matrix, exact Slater minimization is left open but an asymptotic lower bound S(rho_12) >= 2 log n + O(1) is established, with Yang pairing states explaining the spectral obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Squashed entanglement and Slater extensions
proof_stages.summary: Squashed entanglement is defined through extensions and strong subadditivity, the Slater minimizer is conjectured, and an n/2-particle reduced Slater extension gives an O(1/n)-scale upper bound showing different behavior from entanglement of formation.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative monotonicity of relative entropy
proof_stages.summary: The talk then proves a separate strengthened data-processing inequality for Umegaki relative entropy, replacing Golden-Thompson by a three-matrix integral inequality and deriving an explicit lower bound and equality condition.

examples.name: Bosonic product condensate
examples.note: The unentangled product condensate psi=phi(x_1)...phi(x_n) motivates the search for a fermionic analogue, namely Slater determinants as the least-entangled antisymmetric states.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives the condensate as the bosonic product-state analogue and states it is unentangled, but does not compute or derive anything on that example.

passes.reason: A fully factorized condensate has zero entanglement and maximal one-orbital occupation, used as the bosonic baseline.
A bosonic product condensate is a completely factorized all-particles-in-one-mode state, giving the zero-entanglement/minimal-condensation extreme used as contrast rather than as a refutation.
All bosons occupy one orbital, giving a maximally regular rank-one product with zero entanglement and showing that bosonic positive entanglement bounds or condensation-as-entanglement fail.
a fully factorized condensate has all bosons in one orbital and zero entanglement, the minimal uncorrelated bosonic case used only as a contrast
A rank-one symmetric product with all bosons in one orbital is the zero-entanglement/minimal condensate case, used as contrast rather than refutation.
A rank-one symmetric tensor-power condensate has no correlations/entanglement and attains the zero/minimal bosonic entropy, used as contrast.
a rank-one all-bosons-in-one-orbital product condensate has zero entanglement/minimal correlation and is used as the separable bosonic baseline, not as a refutation
A bosonic product condensate is the collapsed all-particles-in-one-orbital zero-entanglement extreme used as a contrast, not as a refutation.
Fully symmetric rank-one tensor-power condensate with zero/minimal entanglement, used as a benchmark rather than a refutation.
A rank-one symmetric tensor power is a maximally condensed, zero-correlation endpoint used as a contrast to fermionic entanglement rather than to refute the main claim.
A fully factorized symmetric tensor power with all bosons in one orbital is a zero-entanglement/minimal-entropy condensate used as a contrast case.
a bosonic product condensate is a completely separable rank-one product state attaining zero entanglement, used only as a contrast to fermions

examples.name: Finite box and ignored degrees of freedom
examples.note: The container with outside electrons and irrelevant color/flavor/spin labels illustrates why the relevant particle number and entropy are determined by the chosen algebra of observables or coarse graining.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses the finite box and ignored degrees of freedom as a conceptual motivation for finite n and observables, without a concrete mathematical calculation or construction on the example.

passes.reason: The finite observable box with ignored degrees of freedom is an ordinary coarse-grained subsystem, invoked to defeat the naive counting of all universal or irrelevant degrees of freedom.
The finite box with a restricted observable algebra is an ordinary finite-volume/coarse-grained subsystem used to explain why only observed degrees of freedom count.
The finite-box restriction to observed degrees of freedom is structurally ordinary but is used to refute the naive inclusion of all irrelevant degrees of freedom in entropy or particle count.
the finite-box/restricted-observable example is structurally ordinary but is used to rule out the naive counting of all irrelevant or external degrees of freedom
The finite observable box is structurally ordinary but is used to reject the naive idea that all unobserved degrees of freedom should count toward n or entropy.
The finite-volume/local observable algebra with ignored variables is an ordinary coarse-grained modeling restriction used to explain finite n.
the finite box with a restricted observable algebra is an ordinary coarse-grained physical setup used to motivate finite n and observable-dependent entropy
The finite box with restricted observables is an ordinary coarse-grained subsystem used to explain the meaning of finite n.
Ordinary finite-volume restricted-observable setup, used to show that including all unobserved degrees of freedom would make entropy or n nonsensical.
The finite-box/restricted-observable setup is structurally ordinary but is used to show the naive inclusion of all irrelevant degrees of freedom makes entropy and particle number notions fail.
The ordinary finite-box/restricted-observable setup is invoked to show that naively counting inaccessible degrees of freedom would make entropy nonsensical.
the finite-box/restricted-observable setup is used to show the naive inclusion of all unobserved degrees of freedom would make entropy nonsensically large

examples.name: Bell e-bit
examples.note: The Bell state (|00>+|11>)/sqrt(2) is used as the standard unit of bipartite entanglement before the talk narrows to entropic bounds.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes/describes the Bell e-bit state and its role as a unit of entanglement, but does not work out any quantity for it.

passes.reason: A Bell e-bit is a maximally entangled two-qubit pure state, used as the standard unit of entanglement.
A Bell e-bit is a maximally entangled two-qubit state with equal Schmidt coefficients, serving as the standard unit of entanglement rather than a counterexample.
The equal-Schmidt Bell pair is a highly regular maximally entangled two-qubit state, introduced as the standard unit of entanglement rather than as an obstruction.
the Bell e-bit is the canonical two-qubit maximally entangled state with equal Schmidt coefficients, used as the basic unit rather than to refute a claim
The Bell e-bit has equal Schmidt coefficients and is maximally entangled for two qubits, serving as the canonical unit of entanglement.
The Bell e-bit is a maximally entangled two-qubit state with maximally mixed marginals and Bell-state symmetry, used as a resource unit.
the Bell e-bit is the standard maximally entangled two-qubit state, introduced as the unit of entanglement rather than as a counterexample
A Bell e-bit is a maximally entangled two-qubit state serving as the standard unit of entanglement.
Maximally entangled and highly symmetric two-qubit Bell state, introduced as the standard e-bit currency.
The Bell e-bit is a highly regular maximally entangled two-qubit state used as the standard unit of entanglement, not as a disproof.
The Bell e-bit is the maximally entangled and highly regular two-qubit pure state introduced as the standard unit of entanglement.
the Bell e-bit is the standard maximally entangled two-qubit pure state, introduced as the basic unit of entanglement rather than to refute a claim

examples.name: Yang pairing state
examples.note: Yang's paired superposition of Slater determinants gives a two-body eigenvalue of order 1/n but also many tiny eigenvalues, showing why largest-eigenvalue intuition alone cannot yield the two-body entropy minimum.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly constructs Yang’s pairing state from paired basis vectors and Slater sums, then analyzes its largest two-body eigenvalue, entropy, and entanglement estimates.

passes.reason: Yang’s uniform pair state saturates the maximal two-body-density eigenvalue and serves as an adversarial case against Slater-like entropy intuition.
Yang’s paired fermion state is a highly regular family that attains the maximal possible large two-body density-matrix eigenvalue and is used to defeat the naive Slater-like eigenvalue picture.

Yang’s paired equal-superposition family is highly regular and saturates the maximal two-body-density-matrix eigenvalue, serving as the enemy case to naive Slater-like eigenvalue or entropy intuition.
Yang’s equal-pairing fermion state is a highly regular family saturating the large two-body-eigenvalue bound and serves as the enemy case to naive entropy expectations
Yang’s equal-pair superposition is highly regular and attains the maximal two-body density-matrix eigenvalue, used as an enemy case to naive two-body entropy intuition.
Yang’s equal-pairing state is highly regular and saturates the maximal two-body-RDM eigenvalue, defeating the naive low-entropy/eigenvalue picture.
Yang's pairing state is a highly regular pair superposition saturating the largest two-body-density-matrix eigenvalue bound and is used as the problematic foil to naive entropy expectations
Yang’s pairing state is a highly regular family attaining the large two-body eigenvalue bound and used as an enemy case against naive low-entropy intuition.
Pair-symmetric equal superposition saturating Yang’s largest two-body-density eigenvalue bound, used as the enemy case for naive Slater-like spectral intuition.
Yang's equal-pairing family is a highly regular state attaining the maximal two-body-density-matrix eigenvalue and is used as an adversarial example against Slater-like spectral intuition.
Yang’s uniform pairing state is a highly regular family saturating the maximal two-body-density-matrix eigenvalue and serving as an enemy case to naive low-entropy expectations.

Yang’s pairing state is an equal-pair superposition that attains the maximal possible large eigenvalue of the two-body density matrix and serves as an adversarial case to naive Slater-like expectations
